title: Maximum Number of Balloons
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C folder with the minimum-memory champion. The recommended and fastest-runtime proposal is implemented in C++; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The memory objective needs only five target-letter counters while scanning a null-terminated char* once. C minimizes explicit auxiliary storage and avoids string/container/runtime metadata beyond the platform input.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Best for the recommended/runtime sibling, but its 26-slot direct-address table intentionally spends more stack state for a branch-regular scan.
Selected. Five scalar counters and a pointer scan are the smallest practical auxiliary state for this fixed-word counting problem.
Native and memory-safe, but wrapper/slice metadata and bounds-check machinery do not reduce state below the C version.
String/slice handling and runtime metadata are heavier than the scalar C implementation.
Managed string/object overhead and runtime baseline are unsuitable for the least-memory axis.
VM strings and dictionary/counter objects are dominated by five native counters.

variants.approachJustification: Scan the string once, increment only b/a/l/o/n counters, halve the doubled letter counts, and return the minimum normalized count.

reasoningSummary: The fixed target word requires only five letter frequencies. Counting target letters once and taking min(b, a, l/2, o/2, n) reaches the input-read lower bound with the smallest auxiliary state.
